content:
Is it possible for a built-in module to unload itself? Functions marked __init get unloaded after init even for built-ins, so clearly it's not entirely impossible to unload code.

Use-case is a built-in module that's only used conditionally depending on a kernel parameter. When it's not used, I just want to unload all of the module's machine code so it doesn't take up ram.
Isn't this already done in free_initmem()?
I also want to free the non-init stuff
Ah, I see.
How much code are we talking about? For data, I think we could just use kalloc() which allocates on-demand. For memory that code takes, wouldn't it usually be relatively small?
I think the driver is 0.9 MB

Like, I want the init function to check a boolean, and if it's set to true then it currently just returns from init without initializing anything. When that happens, I want the module's machine code to go away because we're not going to use it.

Yeah, I know, it's not impossible, but it's complicated: you will need to make sure the code of the module get loaded into a particular area that you could do unmap later. Usually people just use loadable modules for that purpose, since it won't complicate the kernel loading process.
We already have a separate object file that has the logic for setting the boolean used to choose a driver. Could I put some logic in there?
Or would it require surgery on the driver's object file?

IIUC, built-in functions will be linked into the vmlinux, so to unmap the code section, you will need to mark them (similar to __init) somehow, and link script can put the information into the init code.

Before we do that, why don't loadable modules work for you? You will just need to put it in initramfs and load them if needed.
We found out that the partition that holds the modules is using some disc encryption logic, but the thing that is able to decrypt it depends on the driver already being loaded.
Usually bootloaders will handle the decryption, but I guess your case is special. IIUC, you could also put the initramfs into the kernel itself.
I mean, we may be able to put the module in another partition that is loaded earlier, but that essentially involves adding an entirely new kind of module.
I'm just trying to understand our options.

What I suggest is: https://docs.kernel.org/driver-api/early-userspace/early_userspace_support.html#cpio-archive-method

You can put a initramfs (which contains a module) into the final kernel image. In this way, you don't need to worry about the decrytion of the other partition.
And needless to say, after boot, kernel can free the memory that the initramfs takes and you can also unload the module you put it in the intramfs.
And so in that case, init would need to load the module from the new initramfs manually?
init is just a normal userspace program, you can program it to load the new module automatically.
Yes, that's fine.
Okay, well, it sounds like the built-in route requires accepting the cost even when not used.

I haven't done this before, but in theory, this is how it works.

Normally, a bootloader will set up the kernel image and the initramfs via something like:

linux   /vmlinuz-linux root=UUID=258c5484-fbc9-413e-b6f7-072d6081e264 rw  loglevel=3 quiet
initrd  /initramfs-linux.img

but actually, if you're creating the vmlinuz file, you can put a initramfs into it. So you won't need to rely on bootloaders' filesystem driver to load the initramfs, because it's already in the ram (loaded along with kernel).
I mean, we already have other partitions that are mounted early enough.
Ok, so you can put the module there if you want to avoid to load it.
It raises issues with updatability though, since the intent is that you can update the kernel without changing these partitions.
Anyway, gotta go.
I mean it's only 0.9M memory ;-)

The thing with the init section is that you cannot use it after initialization. What you need is different, and to my knowledge there is no such feature upstream. You can absolutely do it, as Boqun says. Make a section similar to the init section, but conditionally free it later. It probably requires quite a bit of surgery.

Alice Ryhl said:

And so in that case, init would need to load the module from the new initramfs manually?

Yes, this is how most PC and server systems work. You load a kernel and an initial in-memory file system. This file system has the init user space program and some drivers that may be required to mount the root partition. The init program then tries to decide what modules to load, loads them, and tries to mount the root file system.

Linking in all modules for all possible root file systems would make a huge kernel image that would take long to load and initialize. Baking in only the right ones is faster, but requires precise knowledge of the system you are booting. That would not work for something like a generic Ubuntu kernel that needs to boot on a wide selection of systems. The in-memory root file system is the halfway point.
